title: Engineered ADARs enable precision A-to-G base editing of DNA - Nature Biotechnology
text:
Download PDF

AbstractAdenine base editors (ABEs), which enable A•T-to-G•C base editing, have emerged as a powerful tool with potential therapeutic applications. However, conventional ABEs suffer from bystander nucleotide conversions, limiting their utility for precise editing. Here we present a single-nucleotide resolution ABE (snuABE) created by fusing a nickase Cas9, nCas9-H840A, with the deaminase domain of ADAR (adenosine deaminase acting on RNA), which acts on DNA:RNA hybrids, instead of TadA, which acts on single-stranded DNA in conventional ABEs. snuABE requires a target-adenine guide RNA (tagRNA) that introduces a mismatch at the target adenine, enabling highly specific A-to-G editing by ADAR. Engineering ADAR from Pediculus humanus using the in silico protein evolution algorithm EvolvePro, along with 3′-end protection of the tagRNA, enhanced snuABE activity, yielding a median efficiency of 5.4% and a maximum efficiency of 50.0% across 32 targets in HEK293T cells. snuABE exhibits no detectable DNA off-target editing at predicted off-target or orthogonal R-loop sites, highlighting its potential as a precise and safe base-editing technology.

MainThe CRISPR–Cas system has been widely used for genetic engineering with high efficacy. CRISPR nucleases are designed to introduce DNA double-strand breaks (DSBs) at target sites in a guide RNA (gRNA)-dependent manner, primarily resulting in gene disruption1. However, this strategy is not suitable for many genetic diseases that require gene correction or base conversion. Additionally, CRISPR nuclease-induced DSBs can lead to serious safety concerns, including large DNA deletions2,3, chromosomal aberrations4 and p53-mediated cell death5.As an alternative, base editors (BEs) have gained notable attention because they enable the modification of one or a few nucleotides with high editing efficiency, without inducing DNA DSBs6. BEs typically consist of a nickase Cas9 (nCas9-D10A) fused to a specific deaminase that catalyzes nucleotide conversions. However, BEs still face notable limitations, which have been partially addressed through several strategies: (1) gRNA-dependent off-target editing in the genome7, which can be reduced by using high-fidelity Cas proteins and modified gRNAs8; (2) gRNA-independent off-target editing in the genome9,10 and transcriptome11,12,13, which can be mitigated using engineered deaminases13,14; and (3) unintended base conversions near the target site (bystander editing) within the editing window, which can be minimized using BE variants with narrower activity windows15. However, despite substantial efforts, completely eliminating bystander editing remains a challenge and often hampers functional recovery16. Furthermore, a trade-off exists between editing efficiency and specificity; BEs with enhanced editing activity typically show increased bystander editing. These limitations underscore the need for the development of a highly precise base-editing tool that can achieve accurate gene correction without bystander effects.ADARs (adenosine deaminases acting on RNA) selectively recognize and deaminate mismatched adenosines to inosines (A-to-I editing) in double-stranded RNA, a process that can alter protein function, affect RNA stability and influence gene expression17. Leveraging this property, several RNA adenine base-editing tools such as CIRTS, LEAPER, RESTORE, REPAIR and RESCUE have been developed18,19,20,21,22. However, to the best of our knowledge, ADAR has not yet been applied for DNA adenine base editing in mammalian cells. In this study, we introduce a single-nucleotide-resolution adenine BE, termed snuABE, in which the deaminase domain of ADAR (ADARdd) is fused to the N terminus of nCas9-H840A. Unlike conventional ABEs that use single gRNAs (sgRNAs), snuABE uses a specialized gRNA, target-adenine gRNA (tagRNA), which creates a mismatch at the target adenine site. For a given DNA target, snuABE can selectively convert specific adenines by designing tagRNAs that introduce unique target-specific mismatches. Notably, the overall editing efficiency of snuABE was enhanced using EvolvePro23, an artificial intelligence (AI)-driven directed evolution framework. Furthermore, snuABE demonstrated extremely low levels of DNA off-target editing activity, highlighting its therapeutic potential as a precise and safe base-editing tool.ResultsOperating concept of snuABE using ADAR and a dedicated gRNAAlthough ADAR typically acts on double-stranded RNA (Supplementary Fig. 1a)17, a previous study showed that it can deaminate DNA adenines within DNA:RNA duplexes in vitro24. On the basis of this finding, we hypothesized that ADAR could be repurposed for DNA adenine editing in human cells, provided that the target adenine is flipped out in a DNA:RNA hybrid conformation (Fig. 1a). To test this, we designed six constructs in which human ADAR2dd (hADAR2dd) containing a hyperactive substitution (E488Q)25 was fused to the N or C terminus of various Cas9 variants, including nCas9-H840A, nCas9-D10A and catalytically inactive Cas9 (dCas9) bearing both D10A and H840A substitutions (Fig. 1b). To create a specific mismatch at the target adenine, we engineered a tagRNA by extending the 3′-end of a conventional sgRNA. We next evaluated the six snuABE constructs using a tagRNA designed to introduce a mismatch at the 12th adenine of the FANCF locus in HEK293T cells. While ABE8e, the representative ABE variant, edited multiple adenines at positions 12, 13 and 18 with efficiencies of up to 61.0%, snuABE exhibited lower but selective editing activity (1.1%) exclusively at the 12th adenine (Fig. 1c). These results confirmed that snuABE can function in human cells with high positional specificity. This initial version was named snuABE1.0.Fig. 1: Conceptualization of snuABE using ADAR and cross-species ADAR screening.Full size imagea, Illustration comparing conventional ABEs and snuABEs, the latter using an ADARdd. b, Schematics of conventional ABE8e and snuABEs based on hADAR2dd. c, Comparison of editing efficiencies at the human FANCF locus using the constructs shown in b. d, Editing efficiency comparison of different ADAR proteins tested at the human AAVS1 and FANCF loci. Cell values in heat maps represent the mean, while bar graphs show the mean ± s.d. with error bars (n = 3 independent biological replicates). All experiments were performed in HEK293T cells and tagRNAs were constructed to have a 24-nt extension sequence at the 3′ end.ADAR enzymes were screened to identify variants with improved editing activity. Given that ADARs are found across a wide range of eukaryotes, 22 ADARdd orthologs from 20 different species, including primates, insects and marine organisms, were retrieved from the National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI) database. Previous studies reported that substituting a specific glutamic acid residue with glutamine (for example, E488Q in hADAR2dd) enhances deaminase activity26. Therefore, we introduced analogous E-to-Q substitutions at conserved residues across all 22 ADARdd orthologs (Fig. 1d and Supplementary Fig. 1). Among the tested ADARdds, the deaminase domain from Pediculus humanus (PhADARdd) demonstrated superior editing activity at both the FANCF target (0.7%) and the AAVS1 safe-harbor locus (6.0%) (Fig. 1d). This improved version was named snuABE2.0.3′-end protection of tagRNA increases snuABE editing efficiencyAlthough snuABE demonstrated higher specificity than ABE8e, its relatively low editing efficiency prompted further optimization. To improve the performance of snuABE2.0, we adopted several strategies previously used to develop the optimized prime-editing system, PEmax27. We generated snuABE2.1 by incorporating the SV40 internal nuclear localization signal (NLS), introducing two substitutions (R221K and N394K) into nCas9-H840A and adding a C-terminal c-Myc NLS (Fig. 2a). Given the structural similarity between tagRNA and the prime-editing gRNA (pegRNA), we hypothesized that protecting the 3′-end of tagRNA might further enhance editing efficiency28. To test this, we coexpressed full-length La or truncated La(1–194) protein along with snuABE2.1. At both the FANCF and the AAVS1 target sites, snuABE2.1 showed improved editing efficiency over snuABE2.0 and coexpression with either form of La protein further enhanced this activity (up to 8.6% for AAVS1 and 5.1% for FANCF) (Fig. 2b,c). Encouraged by these results, we next directly fused the truncated La(1–194) domain to various positions within snuABE2.0, including the N terminus, internal linker region and C terminus (Fig. 2a). The highest editing efficiency was observed when La(1–194) was fused to the C terminus, reaching 16.5% for AAVS1 and 8.5% for FANCF, which was higher than with separate coexpression of La(1–194) (Fig. 2b,c). This optimized version was designated snuABE3.0. Following on from snuABE3.0, we developed snuABE3.1 by applying additional PEmax-inspired modifications, including the SV40 internal NLS, the R221K and N394K substitutions in nCas9-H840A and a C-terminal c-Myc NLS, as implemented in snuABE2.1 (Fig. 2a). This final construct achieved robust editing efficiencies of 20.0% for AAVS1 and 12.1% for FANCF (Fig. 2b,c).Fig. 2: Engineering of snuABEs and target adenine selectivity through tagRNA switching.Full size imagea, Engineering schemes of snuABE constructs from versions 1.0–3.1. b,c, Comparison of editing efficiencies at the human AAVS1 (b) and FANCF (c) loci using constructs from a. d, Comparison of snuABEs with conventional ABEs at the endogenous DNMT1 locus. ABEx1–ABEx4 (ABEx1: V28C, ABEx2: L34W, ABEx3: M151E and ABEx4: V28C;M151E) were used with agRNAs, other ABEs were used with sgRNAs and snuABEs were used with tagRNAs. Cell values in heat maps represent the mean and bar graphs display the mean ± s.d. with error bars (n = 3 independent biological replicates). All experiments were performed in HEK293T cells.We next assessed whether snuABE could selectively edit individual adenines at a given target site by introducing position-specific mismatches using tailored tagRNAs. For this purpose, we designed two tagRNAs targeting either the 13th or 15th adenine at the same site in DNMT1. In parallel, we evaluated a panel of conventional ABEs, including ABEmax, ABE8e, ABE8eWQ, ABE9 and recently developed bystander-reduced variants ABEx1–ABEx4 (refs. 15,29,30,31,32). While all conventional ABEs and ABEx variants edited multiple adenines within their activity window, snuABE3.1 demonstrated clear single-nucleotide resolution. With the tagRNA targeting the 13th adenine, snuABE3.1 efficiently edited the 13th adenine (5.3%) while sparing the 15th (0.5%). Conversely, the 15th adenine-specific tagRNA yielded high editing at the 15th position (6.5%), with minimal editing at the 13th position (1.1 %) (Fig. 2d). These results strongly support the unique single-nucleotide selectivity of snuABE3.1.Characterization of optimized conditions of snuABE3.1We next investigated various characteristics of snuABE3.1, including the optimal tagRNA length, base-editing purity, editing window and sequence motif preference. When testing tagRNAs with 3′ extensions ranging from 15 to 69 nt (Supplementary Table 1), the highest editing efficiencies were consistently observed with tagRNA lengths between 24 and 33 nt (Fig. 3a and Extended Data Fig. 1). Similar to conventional ABE8e, A-to-G conversion was the predominant outcome at target sites, with minimal nonspecific base conversions (Fig. 3b). Furthermore, snuABE3.1 induced minimal insertion and deletion (indel) events (<1.0%), suggesting that snuABE3.1 has a low risk of causing genomic instability. To investigate base-pairing requirements for efficient editing, we tested various counter-RNA bases at the position opposite the target DNA adenine in the mismatch. Only cytosine led to efficient A-to-G conversion, while other bases contributed little or nothing to editing activity (Fig. 3c), consistent with previous observations in RNA editing20. To identify the effective editing window, cells were treated with snuABE3.1 using 32 tagRNAs targeting adenines at different positions. The highest editing efficiencies were consistently observed when adenines were located between positions 12 and 18 (Fig. 3d and Extended Data Fig. 2). Notably, when adenines at positions 18 and 19 were targeted, unintended bystander editing occurred at adenines located within positions 12 and 14 (Extended Data Fig. 2j–l), further supporting a confined editing window spanning positions 12–16.Fig. 3: Functional characterization of snuABE in genomic and transcriptomic contexts.Full size imagea, Comparison of snuABE editing efficiency based on tagRNA length at the human AAVS1 locus. b, Indel frequency and purity of the edited target adenine derived from a. c, Editing efficiency comparison based on the base opposite the target adenine at the AAVS1 and FANCF loci. The dash in the x axis indicates a bulge (or no mismatch). d, Effective targeting range of snuABE across 13 distinct sites within the AAVS1 locus. e, Editing efficiency across all three-base motifs (NAN) at 32 endogenous loci. Sequence logos were generated using the ‘seqLogo’ package in R, based on normalized base-editing efficiency rather than nucleotide frequency. f, Violin plot summarizing the editing-efficiency distribution across NAN motifs from the dataset shown in e. g, Total base conversion rates of ABE8e and snuABE3.1 at six off-target sites identified by Cas-OFFinder. h, On-target and off-target editing activities of ABE8e and snuABE3.1 measured using the orthogonal R-loop assay. On-target data represent A-to-G editing at the AAVS1 12AAC target measured in experiments paired with each of the six dSaCas9-induced R-loop sites, with three independent biological replicates per site, yielding n = 18 data points per editor. Off-target data represent A-to-G conversion levels at adenines located at the same relative positions within the corresponding R-loop regions across the six dSaCas9-induced sites, each tested in three independent biological replicates. The corresponding site-level data are shown in Extended Data Fig. 5b. i, Comparison of A-to-I editing effects on seven endogenous mRNA sites using full-length hADAR1, hADAR2, snuABE1.0 and snuABE3.1. j, Whole-RNA sequencing analysis of A-to-I editing ratios for ABE8e, H840A, snuABE1.0 and snuABE3.1, each performed in a single replicate. The cell values in the heat map represent mean values; bar graphs show the mean ± s.d. with error bars (n = 3 independent biological replicates). All experiments were performed in HEK293T cells.ADAR is known to exhibit sequence preferences for RNA editing, favoring a 5′ U and 3′ G adjacent to the target adenine33. Therefore, we similarly examined the sequence context preferences of snuABE3.1. Using 32 target sites with varied 5′ and 3′ flanking sequences at adenines positioned between 12 and 15, we found that snuABE3.1 generally preferred A or T at the 5′ position and C or G at the 3′ position relative to the target adenine (Fig. 3e). Across these arbitrarily selected targets in HEK293T cells, the median editing efficiency was 2.1% (Fig. 3f), suggesting that snuABE3.1 is highly target-dependent. We also observed that the broader surrounding sequence context affects snuABE3.1 activity. For example, when editing an AAVS1-derived target containing a nonpreferred motif (5′-GAC-3′), the editing efficiency was 17.3%, slightly lower but comparable to that of the preferred motif (5′-AAC-3′), which yielded 20.8% editing (Extended Data Fig. 3). However, altering the flanking distal sequences while retaining the preferred 5′-AAC-3′ led to a marked reduction in efficiency to 6.9%. These results indicate that, while snuABE3.1 exhibits a clear motif preference, its editing efficiency is also strongly influenced by the broader sequence context.To improve the relatively low editing efficiency and motif dependency of snuABE, we tested a series of tagRNA modifications, such as adding one or multiple mismatch sequences near the target adenine, inspired by strategies to enhance ADAR-mediated RNA editing. However, in most cases, DNA editing efficiency was substantially reduced. We speculate that these results arise from fundamental mechanistic differences between the RNA–RNA duplex, the natural substrate of ADAR, and the DNA–RNA hybrid structure required for snuABE activity (Extended Data Fig. 4).Determination of off-target effects on genome and transcriptomeConventional ABEs have been reported to induce both gRNA-dependent7 and gRNA-independent off-target edits in the genome9,10, as well as gRNA-independent RNA deamination events11,12,13,34. We investigated both tagRNA-dependent and tagRNA-independent off-target effects of snuABE3.1 on the genome and transcriptome. To assess tagRNA-dependent off-target activity, we tested snuABE3.1 at the six most likely off-target sites for AAVS1, which include sequences with up to two mismatches or one bulge. While ABE8e showed substantial off-target editing at all six sites, no detectable off-target DNA editing was observed with snuABE3.1 (Fig. 3g and Extended Data Fig. 5a). To evaluate tagRNA-independent genomic deamination, we applied the orthogonal R-loop assay35. This assay involves coexpression of BEs with a catalytically inactive Staphylococcus aureus Cas9 (dSaCas9), which forms artificial R-loop structures, thereby revealing potential off-target deaminase activity independent of gRNA targeting. Notably, while ABE8e induced strong off-target editing at all dSaCas9 binding sites, snuABE3.1 showed no detectable off-target editing at any of the tested sites (Extended Data Fig. 5b,c), despite displaying comparable on-target editing efficiency at AAVS1 (Fig. 3h).RNA A-to-I editing, mediated by ADARs, is a frequent and functionally important event in human cells, having a role in post-transcriptional gene regulation17. Given this, we hypothesized that snuABE3.1 might induce gRNA-independent RNA deamination. To evaluate this, we first analyzed six well-characterized endogenous ADAR hotspot sites in human mRNAs36. Although the editing levels were lower than those typically observed with native hADARs, snuABE3.1 still induced detectable A-to-I conversions at several of these sites (Fig. 3i). To further assess transcriptome-wide RNA off-target activity, we performed whole-transcriptome RNA sequencing in cells expressing snuABE1.0 (containing hADAR2dd), snuABE3.1 (containing PhADARdd), ABE8e (positive control) and nCas9(H840A) (negative control). The results revealed that snuABE3.1 exhibited global A-to-I RNA-editing frequencies higher than snuABE1.0 but lower than those observed with ABE8e (Fig. 3j), indicating that snuABE3.1 possesses moderate RNA deamination activity.Enhancing editing efficiency of snuABE through AI-based directed evolutionAlthough snuABE3.1 demonstrated usable levels of editing efficiency, its overall activity remained lower than that of conventional ABEs and its sequence motif preference posed limitations. To address these issues, we focused on engineering the PhADARdd of snuABE3.1. Structural prediction of PhADARdd bound to a DNA:RNA hybrid using AlphaFold3 (ref. 37) revealed a prominent N-terminal flap (Fig. 4a). We hypothesized that, while this flap might be important for the full-length PhADAR protein, it may not be essential for the catalytic PhADARdd. Therefore, we generated a truncated version of PhADARdd lacking the ten N-terminal residues, which resulted in enhanced editing activity (Fig. 4b). This improved variant was named snuABE4.0.Fig. 4: Engineering and optimization of snuABE variants through AI-assisted evolution.Full size imagea, Predicted structure of the PhADARdd bound to a DNA:RNA hybrid, modeled using AlphaFold3. DNA is shown in orange; RNA is shown in blue. b, Editing efficiencies of truncated N-terminal flap variants of snuABE3.1. Each point represents a single replicate performed on a distinct target. c, Schematic of the directed evolution strategy using EvolvePro. Each point indicates the mean of two independent biological replicates per target. d, Fold change in editing efficiency from predicted substitutions identified by EvolvePro, tested across five endogenous targets. e, Schematic representations of snuABE4.0, snuABE4.1, snuABE4.1n, snuABE4.2 and snuABE4.2n, incorporating an additional nicking gRNA and MLH1-SB fusion. f, Motif-wide editing efficiencies of snuABE3.1 and snuABE4.1 across nine targets. g, Violin plot summarizing the editing-efficiency distributions of snuABE3.1, snuABE3.1n, snuABE4.1 and snuABE4.1n. Each data point in g individually represents the mean of each 32 targets presented in Extended Data Fig. 2a,b. h, Fold changes in editing efficiency induced by MLH1-SB, calculated by comparing snuABE4.1-MLH1-SB with snuABE4.1 across five target sites in each cell line (HEK293T, HEK293, HeLa and U2OS) in Supplementary Fig. 2. Editing efficiencies at each target site were measured from three independent biological replicates and each data point represents the fold change calculated from the mean editing efficiency of snuABE4.1-SB relative to that of snuABE4.1 at the same target site. Statistical significance was determined for each cell line using a two-sided paired t-test comparing snuABE4.1-SB and snuABE4.1 across the five matched target sites (n = 5 paired target sites), without adjustment for multiple comparisons. Exact P values were as follows: HEK293T, P = 0.2944; HEK293, P = 0.0103; HeLa, P = 0.0417; U2OS, P = 0.0783. Asterisks indicate comparisons with P < 0.05. NS, not significant. i, Total base conversion rates of snuABE4.1 at six off-target sites identified by Cas-OFFinder. j, Off-target editing activities of ABE8e and snuABE4.1 as measured by an orthogonal R-loop assay. k, Whole-RNA sequencing analysis of A-to-I editing ratios for ABE8e, H840A, snuABE3.1 and snuABE4.1, each performed in a single replicate. l, Overlap of RNA-seq-identified A-to-I editing sites from ABE8e, H840A, snuABE3.1 and snuABE4.1 with endogenous A-to-I editing sites annotated in the REDIportal database. m,n, A-to-G editing efficiencies of snuABE4.2(n) at multiple loci in patient-derived human fibroblasts (m) and iPS cells (n). The cell values in the heat map represent mean values; bar graphs show the mean ± s.d. with error bars; experiments shown were performed with n = 3 (f,j,n), n = 4 (h) and n = 2 (i,m) independent biological replicates. Unless otherwise indicated, experiments were performed in HEK293T cells.EvolvePro was used to further improve editing efficiency and reduce sequence bias in snuABE4.0. We first selected five endogenous genomic sites with diverse sequence motifs and target A positions (Fig. 3e): 5′-AAT-3′, 5′-AAG-3′, 5′-TAC-3′ and 5′-GAC-3′ at position 12 (with editing efficiencies of 0.36%, 10.2%, 3.9% and 1.02%, respectively) and 5′-AAG-3′ at position 15 (1.2% editing efficiency). In the first round, we generated 187 snuABE variants containing each single random substitution in PhADARdd and evaluated their performance in HEK293T cells. For each target, the top eight substitutions were selected on the basis of predicted editing gains by EvolvePro, resulting in 40 unique variants. These were experimentally tested across all five target sites and the resulting data were used to iteratively retrain EvolvePro to improve prediction accuracy in subsequent rounds (Fig. 4c). Through successive rounds of evolution, we observed a stepwise improvement in editing efficiency. In the final (fifth) round, we used EvolvePro’s combinatorial substitution prediction module to identify the top 20 multimutant variants, each containing 2–5 substitutions predicted to synergistically enhance activity. Experimental validation of these multimutants revealed a substantial gain in editing efficiency, with one variant harboring three substitutions (E438Q, I536S, E637F) showing the highest activity. This optimized version was designated snuABE4.1 (Fig. 4d,e). Notably, snuABE4.1 exhibited generally improved editing efficiency at various sequence contexts, including targets with a 5′-G (Fig. 4f) and overall editing efficiency improved markedly, with a median editing rate of 4.8%, compared to 2.1% observed with snuABE3.1 (Fig. 4g). To gain mechanistic insights into the AI-predicted substitutions, we analyzed the snuABE4.1 variant using AlphaFold3. Structural modeling revealed that the key substitutions (I536S and E637F) are not located within the catalytic core of ADARdd, but rather are concentrated near the DNA–RNA-binding loop (Extended Data Fig. 6). This suggests that these substitutions likely enhance editing activity through stabilization or improved recognition of the DNA:RNA hybrid substrate, rather than directly increasing intrinsic catalytic turnover.MLH1-SB (small binder) and additional nick further enhance editing efficiency of snuABEThe snuABE system uses nCas9-H840A, which introduces a nick on the same DNA strand as the target adenine. This configuration may limit editing efficiency during the repair process. Therefore, similar to the prime-editing system (for example, PE2 and PE3), we hypothesized that introducing an additional nick on the opposite DNA strand could enhance editing efficiency (Fig. 4e)38. To test this, we codelivered nicking gRNAs (ngRNAs) along with either snuABE3.1 or snuABE4.1, generating the modified versions snuABE3.1n and snuABE4.1n, respectively. These systems were tested across 32 target sites with various sequence motifs. The addition of the opposite-strand nick substantially improved editing outcomes, with snuABE4.1n achieving a median editing efficiency of 5.4% (Fig. 4g and Extended Data Fig. 7a,b). To assess the impact of ngRNA position on editing outcomes, we systematically investigated A-to-G editing efficiency and indel formation across multiple ngRNA positions at four endogenous target sites (Extended Data Fig. 7c,d). At all target sites tested, editing efficiency and indel frequency varied widely depending on ngRNA placement location. Notably, certain ngRNA positions supported robust editing while substantially suppressing indel formation, whereas other positions increased indel rates despite comparable editing levels. This ngRNA position-dependent effect was consistently observed across target sites and cell types, suggesting that ngRNA position is a key determinant of indel outcome.We also considered the DNA repair pathways involved in snuABE activity. While conventional BEs primarily engage the base excision repair pathway, we posited that snuABE may also invoke mismatch repair (MMR) because of mismatches introduced on the nicked strand by nCas9-H840A, which could be targeted and corrected by the MMR system. To test this hypothesis, we coexpressed snuABE4.1 with either a dominant-negative MLH1 (MLH1dn) or MLH1-SB, both of which act as MMR inhibitors27,39. These configurations were termed snuABE4.1-MLH1dn and snuABE4.1-SB (also referred to as snuABE4.2) and were tested at the multiple loci across multiple cell lines (HEK293T, HEK293, HeLa and U2OS) (Supplementary Fig. 2). In MMR-deficient HEK293T cells40, neither MMR inhibitor increased editing efficiency. However, in MMR-proficient HEK293, HeLa and U2OS cells, both inhibitors greatly enhanced editing efficiencies (Fig. 4h), indicating that suppression of MMR is beneficial for snuABE performance. Further improvement was achieved by combining ngRNAs with snuABE4.2, generating snuABE4.2n. This configuration led to a robust increase in editing activity across all tested loci (Supplementary Fig. 2).We performed comprehensive DNA and RNA off-target analyses of the evolved snuABE4.1. Similar to snuABE3.1, no detectable DNA off-target editing, whether gRNA dependent or independent, was observed in snuABE4.1 (Fig. 4i,j and Supplementary Fig. 3). In contrast, the total number of RNA off-target editing events increased in snuABE4.1 compared to snuABE3.1 (Fig. 4k). Given the potential concern that ADAR-derived RNA off-target activity could preferentially affect endogenous A-to-I RNA-editing sites and thereby perturb physiological RNA regulation, we assessed the overlap between the observed RNA off-target sites and a curated database of endogenous A-to-I editing sites41 (Fig. 4l). Notably, in both snuABE3.1 and snuABE4.1, the fraction of RNA off-targets overlapping endogenous A-to-I sites increased proportionally to the total RNA off-target burden and no selective enrichment of endogenous A-to-I sites was observed. These results indicate that the increased RNA off-target activity observed in snuABE4.1 reflects an overall increase in RNA-editing activity, rather than selective or biased targeting of endogenous A-to-I RNA-editing sites (Supplementary Fig. 4).To evaluate the potential in clinically relevant contexts, we assessed snuABE4.2n activity at four endogenous sites (AAVS1, RNF2, HEK3 and FANCF) in patient-derived human fibroblasts and at two endogenous sites (AAVS1 and FANCF) in induced pluripotent stem cells (iPS cells). Notably, editing efficiencies were also enhanced upon ngRNA addition, reaching up to 16.3% in fibroblasts and 1.4% in iPS cells, both at the AAVS1 locus (Fig. 4m,n). We also examined the compatibility with protospacer-adjacent motif (PAM)-flexible Cas9 variants to expand the targeting range. To this end, we adopted SpCas9-NG42 (with NG PAM) or SpRY43 (with NRN, NTA and NCK PAMs) variants in snuABE4.2, named SpNG-snuABE4.2 and SpRY-snuABE4.2 respectively, and tested them at multiple target sites in HEK293T cells. Compared to the canonical snuABE4.2 with NGG PAM, the editing efficiencies of the SpNG-snuABE4.2 and SpRY-snuABE4.2 variants were reduced overall; however, SpRY-snuABE4.2 achieved up to 6.0% efficiency at the target site with NAN PAM (Supplementary Fig. 5), indicating that snuABEs are compatible with PAM-flexible Cas9 variants.Precise adenine correction in genetic disease models by snuABETo evaluate the therapeutic potential of snuABE, we first established a HEK293T cell model of von Hippel–Lindau (VHL) syndrome (Fig. 5a)44. To correct the target adenine mutation, three gRNAs were designed: one targeting a 5′-NG-3′ PAM and two targeting 5′-NGG-3′ PAMs. While conventional ABEs exhibited high levels of bystander editing within the editing window, snuABE4.1n achieved bystander-free, pinpoint editing with an efficiency of up to 63.6% (Fig. 5b). These results demonstrate that snuABE can target previously inaccessible genomic sites where conventional ABEs are limited by bystander effects, broadening the scope for therapeutic genome editing.Fig. 5: Application of snuABE variants for therapeutic A-to-G correction at disease-relevant genomic loci.Full size imagea, DNA sequence information of the VHL S111N (c.332G>A) mutation in a VHL syndrome-mimicking disease model using HEK293T cell lines. b, Comparison of editing outcomes between conventional ABEs and snuABEs in the VHL syndrome-mimicking HEK293T cells. c, DNA sequence information of the splicing donor regions in exons 1 and 4 of the PCSK9 gene targeted by snuABEs. d, Evaluation of snuABE-mediated A-to-G editing at the splicing donor regions of the PCSK9 gene in HeLa cells. e, DNA sequence information of the BCL11A +55 enhancer region targeted by snuABEs. f, Evaluation of snuABE-mediated A-to-G editing at the BCL11A +55 enhancer region of the BCL11A gene in K562 cells. g, Schematic of in vivo mouse experiment through the hydrodynamic tail-vein injection. h, Evaluation of snuABE-mediated A-to-G editing at the Gcnt4 intron site in total mouse liver. i, Heat map of total base conversion rates in h. The bar graphs show the mean ± s.d. with error bars (n = 3 independent biological replicates, unless otherwise indicated). In the case of h, n = 1 for the negative control group NC.Low-density lipoprotein (LDL)-related disorders pose a major global health burden and genome editing of PCSK9 has emerged as a promising strategy to reduce LDL levels by disrupting PCSK9 expression45. However, minimizing off-target effects is critical for clinical application. To this end, we investigated the suitability of snuABE, which is characterized by extremely low DNA off-target activity, for precise and safe PCSK9 editing in HeLa cells. Splice donor sites located downstream of exons 1 and 4 in PCSK9 were targeted using snuABE4.2, both with and without additional ngRNAs (Fig. 5c). Robust A-to-G conversions were achieved, reaching up to 10.5% at the exon 1 site and 12.3% at the exon 4 site (Fig. 5d), supporting the clinical applicability of snuABE for treating LDL-associated disorders.We also explored the applicability of snuABE to precise editing of noncoding regulatory elements by targeting the +55 erythroid enhancer of BCL11A, a clinically validated locus for fetal hemoglobin induction46. Using snuABE4.2n, we targeted an adenine within the +55 BCL11A erythroid enhancer (TGN7–9WGATAR motif) using a combination of tagRNA and ngRNA (Fig. 5e). This configuration enabled efficient and highly specific A-to-G conversion with minimal indel formation in both HEK293T and erythroid K562 cells, achieving editing efficiencies of up to 18.1% in K562 cells (Fig. 5f).Lastly, to assess the in vivo applicability of snuABE, we evaluated its editing efficiency in mouse NIH/3T3 cells by targeting the mouse Gcnt4 intron site. snuABE4.1 exhibited a low editing efficiency of approximately 0.9%, whereas ngRNA-assisted snuABE4.1n and snuABE4.2n showed substantially increased efficiencies of 27.0% and 25.7%, respectively (Supplementary Fig. 5). On the basis of these results, we delivered three plasmids constituting snuABE4.2n (that is, snuABE4.2, tagRNA and ngRNA) into mice through hydrodynamic tail-vein injection to enable efficient delivery and base editing in the liver (Fig. 5g). Base-editing outcomes were analyzed in whole liver tissue 7 days after injection. snuABE4.2n induced an average editing efficiency of 2.0% (Fig. 5h), which is comparable to previously reported Cas9-mediated indel frequencies (~2.6%)47 and higher than the efficiency achieved by prime editors (<1%)39. Notably, no bystander editing was detected in adenines adjacent to the target adenine (Fig. 5i).DiscussionAlthough conventional ABEs are widely used because of their ease of application and high editing efficiency, they are notably limited by the frequent occurrence of bystander editing, which constrains their therapeutic utility. Various strategies have been proposed to address this limitation, including TadA engineering14,15,30,48, sgRNA modification49, motif-preferred ABEs25 and the use of accessory proteins50. However, these approaches have shown limited success in achieving precise single-base editing. In this study, we developed snuABE to achieve single-nucleotide-resolution adenine conversion by inducing editing specifically at mismatched adenines formed in DNA:RNA hybrids. This design greatly reduced bystander editing. Furthermore, snuABE exhibited markedly lower DNA off-target activity, likely because of the requirement for perfect complementarity between the tagRNA and the target sequence, similar to the mechanism used by prime editors. These features position snuABE as a promising and safer BE for therapeutic applications. The recent clinical success of ABE-based therapy for carbamoyl-phosphate synthase 1 deficiency highlights the translational potential of BEs and underscores the need for improved precision and safety51.To optimize snuABE up to version 4.2, we adopted strategies from prime editor development because of their similar architecture involving nCas9-H840A and 3′-extended gRNAs52. The additional mutations and NLSs used in PEmax were incorporated into snuABE2.1 (ref. 27). Likewise, introducing an additional nick on the opposite strand (as in the PE3 strategy) and protecting the 3′-end of tagRNA with La protein (as in PE7) contributed to the creation of the snuABE3 variants27,38. Moreover, we applied the AI-based EvolvePro algorithm to engineer the PhADARdd deaminase, generating snuABE4 variants in a manner analogous to the PE6 strategy23,53. MMR suppression through MLH1dn or MLH1-SB further enhanced editing efficiency, paralleling the approaches used in PE4 and PE-SB, and led to the generation of snuABE4.2 (refs. 27,39).Despite these improvements, snuABE4.2 still faces several limitations: (1) lower editing efficiency compared to conventional ABEs such as ABE8e; (2) residual sequence preference around the target adenine; and (3) considerable off-target editing on RNA transcripts. In particular, the major substitutions (I536S and E637F) introduced into snuABE4.1 or snuABE4.2 through AI-based directed evolution not only improved DNA target editing efficiency but also simultaneously increased RNA off-target activity. This is a trade-off between enhanced DNA editing and increased RNA off-target activity. While RNA off-target effects are transient, these issues highlight the need for further refinement. Strategies such as genome-wide CRISPR screening to identify novel cofactors or advanced ADAR engineering, potentially through AI-guided discovery of orthologs, may offer promising paths forward. In addition, it would be beneficial to further develop snuABE to induce cytosine editing without bystander effects.To expand the targeting range of snuABE, compatibility with Cas9 variants or various types of CRISPR effectors is also important. We found that base-editing efficiencies in PAM-flexible contexts by SpNG-snuABE4.2 and SpRY-snuABE4.2 were generally lower than those by the canonical snuABE4.2 with NGG PAM. We speculate that snuABE activity relies not only on Cas9-mediated DNA targeting but also on the formation of an efficient DNA:RNA duplex through stable binding between the tagRNA and the target DNA strand. Therefore, systematic optimization of enzyme kinetics, as demonstrated by the evolution from TadA7.1 to TadA8e, can compensate for suboptimal targeting conditions. Thus, we believe that enzyme-level kinetic engineering is a promising strategy to further enhance snuABE performance in PAM-flexible environments.Nonetheless, snuABE provides a compelling alternative to conventional ABEs, offering high specificity and minimal DNA off-target activity. While prime editors are highly versatile and capable of inducing various types of edits (insertions, deletions and substitutions), their clinical utility is often hindered by the complexity of pegRNA design and typically lower editing efficiency. In contrast, snuABE relies on a relatively simple tagRNA design, requiring only optimization of the 3′ extension length (typically 24–32 nt). Moreover, prime editing restricts editing of sequences located in the PAM-distal region from the nick site, whereas snuABE enables editing within the editing activity window (positions 11–17). In a disease-relevant model of VHL syndrome, snuABE achieved comparable editing efficiencies to ABE8e, with minimized bystander edits. We further demonstrate the in vivo applicability of snuABE through hydrodynamic delivery in mouse liver, where measurable A-to-G editing was achieved without detectable bystander edits. This result supports the therapeutic potential of snuABEs for precise correction in a physiological context. Future optimization of delivery strategies involving lipid nanoparticles and engineered virus-like particles is expected to improve delivery efficiency and editing efficacy in vivo. Collectively, these results highlight the potential of snuABE as a versatile and precise editing system, particularly for genomic loci that are difficult to target with existing ABE technologies.MethodsMolecular cloning of tagRNAs, anchor gRNA (agRNA), sgRNA and ngRNAThe tagRNAs, sgRNAs, and ngRNAs used for plasmid transfection were cloned into the pU6-pegRNA-GG-acceptor plasmid (Addgene, 132777). The sequences of all tagRNAs, sgRNAs, ngRNAs and oligonucleotides used in this study are provided in Supplementary Table 1. The agRNAs used with ABEx1–ABEx4 (ABEx1: V28C, ABEx2: L34W, ABEx3: M151E and ABEx4: V28C;M151E)32 were cloned into the pU6-tevopreq1-GG-acceptor plasmid (Addgene, 174038). Spacers and extensions were synthesized as oligonucleotides (Macrogen), phosphorylated using T4 polynucleotide kinase (enzynomics, M005S) and annealed (37 °C for 30 min and 95 °C for 2 min, before cooling to 25 °C at a 0.1 °C s−1 for 5 min). The Drosophila ADAR sequence was obtained from the HyperADAR control plasmid (Addgene, 166969). ADARdds from other species were synthesized (Twist Bioscience). The PEmax domain used to construct snuABE2.1 was derived from pCMV-PEmax (Addgene, 174820). The human SSB cDNA, encoding La protein, was amplified from HEK293T cDNA by PCR. Total RNA was extracted from HEK293T cells (American Type Culture Collection (ATCC), CRL-11268) using Trizol (Invitrogen, 15596026) and cDNA was synthesized using ReverTra Ace (Toyobo, FSK-101F). The coding sequence was then amplified using KOD plus Neo (Toyobo, TOKOD-401). The snuABE constructs used in this study were deposited to Addgene. For passaging, cells were washed with DPBS (Welgene, LB001-01), detached using 0.25% trypsin–EDTA (Welgene, LS015) and resuspended in fresh medium before being transferred to new plates. Human fibroblasts were maintained in DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS, 1% antibiotics and 1× GlutaMAX supplement (Gibco, 35050061) at 37 °C with 5% CO2. Cells were cultured in T75 flasks (SPL, 70075) with 20 ml of medium and passaged using TrypLE Express (Gibco, 12605010) after washing with DPBS. hiPS cells (CMC-hiPSC-022, Catholic University of Korea) were cultured in Essential 8 medium (Thermo Scientific, A1517001) on 60-mm tissue culture dishes coated with Vitronectin (Gibco, A14700). For passaging, cells were washed with DPBS and detached using Accutase (StemCell Technologies, 07922). After detachment, cells were washed twice with DPBS and transferred to Vitronectin-coated plates in Essential 8 medium supplemented with ROCK inhibitor (Y27632 dihydrochloride; MedChemExpress, HY-10583). hiPS cells were maintained at 37 °C with 5% CO2.Transfection and transduction protocols and genomic DNA extractionFor HEK293T, HEK293, HeLa, U2OS and NIH/3T3 cells, 0.25 × 105 cells were seeded per well in 48-well plates (SPL, 30048) containing 250 µl of DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% antibiotics 1 day before transfection. The following day, plasmid vectors were mixed and transfected using 0.5 µl of jetOPTIMUS (Polyplus, 101000006) per well, according to the manufacturer’s instructions. Typically, 375 ng of snuABE and 125 ng of tagRNA were used per transfection. If required, an additional 41.6 ng of ngRNA was included (541.6 ng in total). When coexpressing additional proteins such as La protein or MLH1dn, 100 ng of plasmid was added, bringing the total to 600 ng. After 36 h, 200 µl of medium was replaced with fresh medium (DMEM, 10% FBS and 1% antibiotics). Cells were harvested 36 h later using 0.25% trypsin–EDTA (Welgene, LS015).For transfection of human fibroblasts, electroporation was performed using the Neon transfection system with the 10-μl kit (Invitrogen, MPK1096). Cells detached using TrypLE Express (Gibco, 12605010) were resuspended in Neon R buffer at a density of 1 × 105 cells per reaction and mixed with 1 μg of total plasmid DNA. Electroporation was carried out at 1,600 V with a 20-ms pulse width and a single pulse. After 72 h, cells were harvested using TrypLE Express.For hiPS cell transfection, 1 × 105 cells were seeded into a 24-well plate. The resulting 50-μl mixture was transfected into the cells. In the second step, 1 µl of the initial PCR product was used as the template with index primers. PCR was conducted with KOD Multi & Epi (Toyobo, KME-101) according to the manufacturer’s instructions under the following cycling conditions: 98 °C for 2 min, followed by 29 cycles of 98 °C for 10 s, 56 °C for 20 s and 68 °C for 30 s, with a final extension at 68 °C for 1 min. A total of 1 × 106 HEK293T cells were resuspended in 100 µl of sucrose potassium PBS (119 mM KCl, 15.1 mM NaCl, 0.144 mM CaCl2, 5 mM MgCl2, 9.98 mM KH2PO4 and 250 mM sucrose, pH 7.4), mixed with 2 µg of vector and subjected to electroporation (1,600 V, 20-ms pulse width, one pulse). The transfected cells were transferred to six-well plates (SPL, 30006) containing 2 ml of DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS and incubated at room temperature for 5 min in a clean bench, followed by incubation at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 24 h. To identify differentially expressed genes (DEGs), statistical analyses were performed on the basis of gene-level count estimates across samples. Genes with nonzero read counts in all samples were retained for downstream analysis. The filtered count data were normalized using the trimmed mean of M values method.Differential expression analysis was conducted using the edgeR package58, with fold change analysis performed under the null hypothesis that no difference exists between groups. P values were adjusted to control the false discovery rate using the Benjamini–Hochberg procedure. For the resulting DEG sets, hierarchical clustering analysis was performed using complete linkage and Euclidean distance as the similarity measure. Gene enrichment, functional annotation and pathway analyses were carried out using gProfiler59.Generation of HEK293T cell linesTo generate HEK293T cells with an artificial motif knock-in, lentiviral transduction was used. The lentiviral vector was constructed by digesting pAX198 (Addgene, 173042) with XbaI and XhoI (New England Biolabs, R0145S and R0146S), following the same cloning strategy used for tagRNAs, agRNAs, sgRNAs and ngRNAs. Lenti-X 293T cells (Takara, 632180) were seeded at 6 × 106 cells per 100-mm cell culture dish (SPL, 20100) and the medium was replaced with fresh DMEM containing 10% FBS the following day. Immediately after medium replacement, a mixture consisting of 9.4 µg of the constructed lentiviral vector, 8.6 µg of psPAX2 (Addgene, 12260), 2.6 µg of MD2.G (Addgene, 12259) and 2.0 µg of pRSV-Rev (Addgene, 12253) was prepared and transfected using 65.7 µl of Transporter 5 transfection reagent (Polysciences, 26008-50), according to the manufacturer’s instructions. Then, 1 day after transfection, the medium was replaced with fresh DMEM containing 10% FBS. Then, 2 days later, the medium containing the viral particles was collected, centrifuged at 500g for 10 min to remove cellular debris and filtered through a 0.45-µm filter (Pall, 4614). The virus was then concentrated using the Lenti-X concentrator (Takara, 631232) following the manufacturer’s protocol. HEK293T cells were infected with the concentrated lentivirus at a multiplicity of infection of 0.3. Finally, 2 days after infection, cells were selected with 3 µg ml−1 puromycin (InvivoGen, ant-pr-1) for 3 days.To generate HEK293T cells harboring the VHLS111N (c.332G>A) mutation, prime editing was performed. Briefly 0.25 × 105 HEK293T cells were transfected with 375 ng of PE7 and 125 ng of the corresponding ngRNA using jetOPTIMUS (Polyplus, 101000006), as described above. Then, 7 days after transfection, cells were diluted and seeded into 96-well plates at a density of one cell per well to establish single-cell-derived clones. After sufficient expansion, individual clones were screened by targeted deep sequencing. Clones exhibiting 100% homozygous editing at the target locus were selected and used for subsequent experiments.Directed evolution through EvolveProDirected evolution using the EvolvePro pipeline was conducted as previously described (https://github.com/mat10d/EvolvePro)23. Approximately 200 random single-point mutations were generated and tested in HEK293T cells across five independent target sites. The editing efficiencies of these variants were measured and used as training input for the initial round of EvolvePro predictions. For each target, the top eight substitutions predicted to exhibit the highest activity were selected, resulting in a total of 40 variants (8 per target × 5 targets), which were then experimentally validated across all five sites in subsequent rounds. This iterative process was repeated over multiple rounds, with EvolvePro models updated using the most recent experimental data in a target-specific manner. In the final round, all mutation data were compiled and the average fold change in editing efficiency across the five targets was calculated. Mutations showing a fold change greater than 1 were retained and used to generate all possible combinatorial variants containing up to four substitutions. These multimutant variants were ranked using EvolvePro and the top 20 predicted variants were selected for final experimental validation. Predicted protein structures were visualized and analyzed using UCSF ChimeraX (version 1.8)60.In vivo delivery of snuABE through hydrodynamic tail-vein injectionAll in vivo experiments were conducted in accordance with protocols approved by the Sungkyunkwan University Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (SKKUIACUC2025-06-64-1). Mice were maintained under specific-pathogen-free conditions at 22 ± 2 °C and 50–60% relative humidity under a 12-h light–dark cycle, with ad libitum access to food and water. C57BL/6J mice (Mus musculus) were purchased from Orient Bio. Mice were maintained under specific-pathogen-free conditions and used at 6–8 weeks of age. Only female mice were used in this study to minimize variability associated with sex-dependent physiological differences and to maintain consistency across hydrodynamic injection experiments.For hydrodynamic liver injection, plasmids diluted in 2 ml of saline were injected into 8–10-week-old C57BL/6 mice through the tail vein within 5–7 s.
